question:
9. Evaluate the definite integrals
(i) \( \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d x}{\sqrt{(x-1)(3-x)}} \),
(ii) \( \int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d x}{e^{x+1}+e^{3-x}} \).
solution:
Solution. Part (i). Since the integrand is not defined at either bound of integration, one should write
\[
\begin{aligned}
& \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d x}{\sqrt{(x-1)(3-x)}}=\lim _{\substack{\epsilon-0+\\
\delta-0+}} \int_{1+\epsilon}^{3-\delta} \frac{d x}{\sqrt{(x-1)(3-x)}} \\
= & \left.\lim _{\substack{\epsilon-0+\\
\delta-0+}} \int_{1+\epsilon}^{3-\delta} \frac{d x}{\sqrt{1-(x-2)^{2}}}=\lim _{\substack{\epsilon-0+\\
\delta-0+}} \arcsin (x-2)\right]_{1+\epsilon}^{3-\delta} \\
= & \lim _{\substack{\epsilon \rightarrow 0+\\
\delta \rightarrow 0+}}[\arcsin (1-\delta)-\arcsin (\epsilon-1)]=\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{2}=\pi .
\end{aligned}
\]

Part (ii). The difficulty here is with the infinite interval of integration. Let \( y=x-1 \); then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\int \frac{d x}{e^{x+1}+e^{3-x}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d x}{e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d y}{e^{y}+e^{-y}} \\
=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{e^{y} d y}{e^{2 y}+1}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \arctan e^{y}+c
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d x}{e^{x+1}+e^{3-x}} & =\lim _{N \rightarrow \infty} \int_{1}^{N} \frac{d x}{e^{x+1}+e^{3-x}} \\
& =\lim _{N \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\arctan e^{N-1}-\arctan e^{0}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]=\frac{\pi}{4 e^{2}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long.question:
9. Evaluate the definite integrals
(i) \( \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d variable}{\sqrt{(variable-1)(3-variable)}} \),
(ii) \( \int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d variable}{e^{variable+1}+e^{3-variable}} \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Part (i). Since the integrand is not defined at either bound of integration, one should write
\[
\begin{aligned}
& \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d variable}{\sqrt{(variable-1)(3-variable)}}=\lim _{\substack{tolerance-0+\\
deviation-0+}} \int_{1+tolerance}^{3-deviation} \frac{d variable}{\sqrt{(variable-1)(3-variable)}} \\
= & \left.\lim _{\substack{tolerance-0+\\
deviation-0+}} \int_{1+tolerance}^{3-deviation} \frac{d variable}{\sqrt{1-(variable-2)^{2}}}=\lim _{\substack{tolerance-0+\\
deviation-0+}} \arcsin (variable-2)\right]_{1+tolerance}^{3-deviation} \\
= & \lim _{\substack{tolerance \rightarrow 0+\\
deviation \rightarrow 0+}}[\arcsin (1-deviation)-\arcsin (tolerance-1)]=\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{2}=\pi .
\end{aligned}
\]

Part (ii). The difficulty here is with the infinite interval of integration. Let \( ordinate=variable-1 \); then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\int \frac{d variable}{e^{variable+1}+e^{3-variable}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d variable}{e^{variable-1}+e^{1-variable}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d ordinate}{e^{ordinate}+e^{-ordinate}} \\
=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{e^{ordinate} d ordinate}{e^{2 ordinate}+1}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \arctan e^{ordinate}+constant
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d variable}{e^{variable+1}+e^{3-variable}} & =\lim _{endpoint \rightarrow \infty} \int_{1}^{endpoint} \frac{d variable}{e^{variable+1}+e^{3-variable}} \\
& =\lim _{endpoint \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\arctan e^{endpoint-1}-\arctan e^{0}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]=\frac{\pi}{4 e^{2}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
9. Evaluate the definite integrals
(i) \( \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d marblestone}{\sqrt{(marblestone-1)(3-marblestone)}} \),
(ii) \( \int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d marblestone}{e^{marblestone+1}+e^{3-marblestone}} \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Part (i). Since the integrand is not defined at either bound of integration, one should write
\[
\begin{aligned}
& \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d marblestone}{\sqrt{(marblestone-1)(3-marblestone)}}=\lim _{\substack{hazelnut-0+\\ buttercup-0+}} \int_{1+hazelnut}^{3-buttercup} \frac{d marblestone}{\sqrt{(marblestone-1)(3-marblestone)}} \\
= & \left.\lim _{\substack{hazelnut-0+\\ buttercup-0+}} \int_{1+hazelnut}^{3-buttercup} \frac{d marblestone}{\sqrt{1-(marblestone-2)^{2}}}=\lim _{\substack{hazelnut-0+\\ buttercup-0+}} \arcsin (marblestone-2)\right]_{1+hazelnut}^{3-buttercup} \\
= & \lim _{\substack{hazelnut \rightarrow 0+\\ buttercup \rightarrow 0+}}[\arcsin (1-buttercup)-\arcsin (hazelnut-1)]=\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{2}=\pi .
\end{aligned}
\]

Part (ii). The difficulty here is with the infinite interval of integration. Let \( lanternpost=marblestone-1 \); then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\int \frac{d marblestone}{e^{marblestone+1}+e^{3-marblestone}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d marblestone}{e^{marblestone-1}+e^{1-marblestone}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d lanternpost}{e^{lanternpost}+e^{-lanternpost}} \\
=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{e^{lanternpost} d lanternpost}{e^{2 lanternpost}+1}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \arctan e^{lanternpost}+driftwood
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d marblestone}{e^{marblestone+1}+e^{3-marblestone}} & =\lim _{rainclouds \rightarrow \infty} \int_{1}^{rainclouds} \frac{d marblestone}{e^{marblestone+1}+e^{3-marblestone}} \\
& =\lim _{rainclouds \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\arctan e^{rainclouds-1}-\arctan e^{0}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]=\frac{\pi}{4 e^{2}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem:
<<<
9. Evaluate the definite integrals
(i) \( \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d constantvalue}{\sqrt{(constantvalue-1)(3-constantvalue)}} \),
(ii) \( \int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d constantvalue}{e^{constantvalue+1}+e^{3-constantvalue}} \).
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. Part (i). Since the integrand is not defined at either bound of integration, one should write
\[
\begin{aligned}
& \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d constantvalue}{\sqrt{(constantvalue-1)(3-constantvalue)}}=\lim _{\substack{massivegap-0+\\
hugechange-0+}} \int_{1+massivegap}^{3-hugechange} \frac{d constantvalue}{\sqrt{(constantvalue-1)(3-constantvalue)}} \\
= & \left.\lim _{\substack{massivegap-0+\\
hugechange-0+}} \int_{1+massivegap}^{3-hugechange} \frac{d constantvalue}{\sqrt{1-(constantvalue-2)^{2}}}=\lim _{\substack{massivegap-0+\\
hugechange-0+}} \arcsin (constantvalue-2)\right]_{1+massivegap}^{3-hugechange} \\
= & \lim _{\substack{massivegap \rightarrow 0+\\
hugechange \rightarrow 0+}}[\arcsin (1-hugechange)-\arcsin (massivegap-1)]=\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{2}=\pi .
\end{aligned}
\]

Part (ii). The difficulty here is with the infinite interval of integration. Let \( staticnumber=constantvalue-1 \); then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\int \frac{d constantvalue}{e^{constantvalue+1}+e^{3-constantvalue}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d constantvalue}{e^{constantvalue-1}+e^{1-constantvalue}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d staticnumber}{e^{staticnumber}+e^{-staticnumber}} \\
=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{e^{staticnumber} d staticnumber}{e^{2 staticnumber}+1}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \arctan e^{staticnumber}+variable
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d constantvalue}{e^{constantvalue+1}+e^{3-constantvalue}} & =\lim _{smallcount \rightarrow \infty} \int_{1}^{smallcount} \frac{d constantvalue}{e^{constantvalue+1}+e^{3-constantvalue}} \\
& =\lim _{smallcount \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\arctan e^{smallcount-1}-\arctan e^{0}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]=\frac{\pi}{4 e^{2}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

garbled_string.question:
9. Evaluate the definite integrals
(i) \( \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{\sqrt{(qzxwvtnp-1)(3-qzxwvtnp)}} \),
(ii) \( \int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{e^{qzxwvtnp+1}+e^{3-qzxwvtnp}} \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Part (i). Since the integrand is not defined at either bound of integration, one should write
\[
\begin{aligned}
& \int_{1}^{3} \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{\sqrt{(qzxwvtnp-1)(3-qzxwvtnp)}}=\lim _{\substack{lkrdmvse-0+\\
wqfjzupk-0+}} \int_{1+lkrdmvse}^{3-wqfjzupk} \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{\sqrt{(qzxwvtnp-1)(3-qzxwvtnp)}} \\
= & \left.\lim _{\substack{lkrdmvse-0+\\
wqfjzupk-0+}} \int_{1+lkrdmvse}^{3-wqfjzupk} \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{\sqrt{1-(qzxwvtnp-2)^{2}}}=\lim _{\substack{lkrdmvse-0+\\
wqfjzupk-0+}} \arcsin (qzxwvtnp-2)\right]_{1+lkrdmvse}^{3-wqfjzupk} \\
= & \lim _{\substack{lkrdmvse \rightarrow 0+\\
wqfjzupk \rightarrow 0+}}[\arcsin (1-wqfjzupk)-\arcsin (lkrdmvse-1)]=\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{2}=\pi .
\end{aligned}
\]

Part (ii). The difficulty here is with the infinite interval of integration. Let \( hjgrksla=qzxwvtnp-1 \); then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\int \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{e^{qzxwvtnp+1}+e^{3-qzxwvtnp}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{e^{qzxwvtnp-1}+e^{1-qzxwvtnp}}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{d hjgrksla}{e^{hjgrksla}+e^{-hjgrksla}} \\
=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \int \frac{e^{hjgrksla} d hjgrksla}{e^{2 hjgrksla}+1}=\frac{1}{e^{2}} \arctan e^{hjgrksla}+plsnrqiw
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{e^{qzxwvtnp+1}+e^{3-qzxwvtnp}} & =\lim _{vmbtczay \rightarrow \infty} \int_{1}^{vmbtczay} \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{e^{qzxwvtnp+1}+e^{3-qzxwvtnp}} \\
& =\lim _{vmbtczay \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\arctan e^{vmbtczay-1}-\arctan e^{0}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{e^{2}}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]=\frac{\pi}{4 e^{2}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let  

(I) For every integer k \geq  2 set  
   I_k := \iint _{[0,1]^k} \frac{dx_1\cdots dx_k}{1-\bigl(x_1x_2\cdots x_k\bigr)^{\,2}}\!.

(II) For every real number s>0 set  
   J(s):=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{x^{\,s-1}\,dx}{e^{x}+e^{-x}}\!.

Throughout \Gamma  denotes the Gamma-function, \zeta  the Riemann zeta-function and  
\beta (s)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{n}}{(2n+1)^{s}} the Dirichlet beta-function.

1. (a) Prove that I_k converges for every k\geq 2.  
 (b) Show that  
   I_k=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(2n+1)^{k}}=(1-2^{-k})\,\zeta(k).  
  Give explicit numerical values for k=3 and k=4.

2. (a) Prove that J(s) converges for every s>0 and that  
   J(s)=\Gamma(s)\,\beta(s).  
 (b) Compute J(3) and J(5) in closed form.

3. (Parametrised variant and higher-order polygamma)  
 Fix a real parameter \alpha >0 and set  
   I_k(\alpha ):=\displaystyle\iint_{[0,1]^{k}}\frac{dx_1\cdots dx_k}{1-\bigl(x_1x_2\cdots x_k\bigr)^{\alpha}}\!.  

 (a) Show that I_k(\alpha ) converges for every k\geq 2.

 (b) Prove that  
   I_k(\alpha )=\alpha^{-k}\,\zeta\!\bigl(k,\tfrac1\alpha\bigr),  
  where \zeta (s,q)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(n+q)^{s}} is the Hurwitz zeta-function.

 (c) Deduce Part 1 (b) by putting \alpha =2 and using the identity  
   \zeta (k,\tfrac12)=(2^{k}-1)\,\zeta(k).

 (d)  Denote by \psi ^{(m)}(z)=\dfrac{d^{m+1}}{dz^{m+1}}\log\Gamma(z) the m-th polygamma function.  
  Show that for every even k=2m\geq 2 one has the exact formula  
   I_{2m}(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-2m}}{(2m-1)!}\;\psi^{(2m-1)}\!\Bigl(\frac1\alpha\Bigr). (*)

  Using the known values  

    \psi ^{(3)}\!\bigl(\tfrac12\bigr)=\pi^{4},  \psi ^{(5)}\!\bigl(\tfrac12\bigr)=8\pi^{6},  

  obtain the explicit closed forms  

    I_4(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-4}}{6}\;\psi^{(3)}\!\Bigl(\dfrac1\alpha\Bigr),  
    I_6(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-6}}{120}\;\psi^{(5)}\!\Bigl(\dfrac1\alpha\Bigr),  

  and verify in particular that  
   I_4(2)=\dfrac{\pi^{4}}{96}, I_6(2)=\dfrac{\pi^{6}}{960}.
kernel_variant.solution:
Standard facts about the Gamma, zeta, beta, Hurwitz-zeta and polygamma functions are freely used.


1.  The basic integral I_k

1 (a) Convergence.  
Put P=(x_1x_2\cdots x_k)^2.  Over the unit cube one has 0\leq P\leq 1 and the only potential
singularity of the integrand 1/(1-P) occurs at the point (1,1,\ldots ,1) where P=1.
Write x_i=1-t_i with t_i\in [0,1].  Then

 1-P=1-\bigl(1-\sum_{i=1}^{k}t_i+O(|t|^{2})\bigr)=\sum_{i=1}^{k}t_i+O(|t|^{2}).

Hence near the singular point the integrand behaves like 1/\sum  t_i, whose
k-dimensional integral over a small cube is finite as soon as k\geq 2.
No other singularities exist, so I_k converges for every k\geq 2.

1 (b) Evaluation.  
Expand 1/(1-P)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}P^{n}, interchange
sum and integral (Tonelli) and compute

 I_k=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\prod_{j=1}^{k}\int_{0}^{1}x^{2n}\,dx
     =\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\Bigl(\frac1{2n+1}\Bigr)^{k}
     =(1-2^{-k})\,\zeta(k).

Numerical cases:

 k=3: I_3=(1-\tfrac18)\zeta(3)=\tfrac78\,\zeta(3).  
 k=4: I_4=(1-\tfrac1{16})\zeta(4)=\tfrac{15}{16}\cdot\frac{\pi^{4}}{90}
         =\dfrac{\pi^{4}}{96}.


2.  The integral  J(s)

2 (a) Convergence.  
Near x=0 we have e^{x}+e^{-x}=2+O(x^{2}), so the integrand behaves like
x^{s-1} and is integrable whenever s>0.
For x\to \infty  the denominator grows like e^{x}, so the integrand behaves like
x^{s-1}e^{-x}, again integrable for every s.

Series representation:
 \frac1{e^{x}+e^{-x}}
     =\frac{e^{-x}}{1+e^{-2x}}
     =\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{n}\,e^{-(2n+1)x},
uniformly on [\varepsilon ,\infty ) for any \varepsilon >0; thus

 J(s)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{n}\int_{0}^{\infty}x^{s-1}e^{-(2n+1)x}\,dx
     =\Gamma(s)\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{n}}{(2n+1)^{s}}
     =\Gamma(s)\,\beta(s).

2 (b) Special values.
 \beta (3)=\pi^{3}/32, \beta (5)=5\pi^{5}/1536,

 \Gamma (3)=2, \Gamma (5)=24,

so J(3)=\pi^{3}/16 and J(5)=5\pi^{5}/64.


3.  The parametrised integrals I_k(\alpha )

3 (a) Convergence.  
The only critical point is (1,\ldots ,1).  Put x_i=1-t_i (t_i\geq 0);
then

 1-\bigl(x_1x_2\cdots x_k\bigr)^{\alpha}
     =1-\exp\!\bigl(\alpha\sum_{i}\log(1-t_i)\bigr)
     =\alpha\sum_{i}t_i+O(|t|^{2}).

Thus the integrand behaves like 1/\sum  t_i, which is k-integrable for k\geq 2.

3 (b) Evaluation through the Hurwitz zeta.  
Expand

 \frac1{1-P^{\alpha}}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}P^{\alpha n}, P=x_1x_2\cdots x_k,

interchange sum and integral and obtain

 I_k(\alpha )=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\Bigl(\frac1{\alpha n+1}\Bigr)^{k}
         =\alpha^{-k}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(n+\frac1\alpha)^{k}}
         =\alpha^{-k}\,\zeta\!\bigl(k,\tfrac1\alpha\bigr).

3 (c) Specialisation \alpha =2.  
Because \zeta (k,\tfrac12)=(2^{k}-1)\,\zeta (k) we recover

 I_k(2)=2^{-k}(2^{k}-1)\,\zeta (k)=(1-2^{-k})\,\zeta (k)

in agreement with Part 1 (b).

3 (d) Even orders and the polygamma function.  
Recall the higher-order
polygamma: \psi ^{(m)}(z)=\dfrac{d^{m+1}}{dz^{m+1}}\log\Gamma(z).
For n\geq 0 one has  

 \psi ^{(n)}(z)=(-1)^{\,n+1}n!\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(r+z)^{\,n+1}}.

Taking n=2m-1 (m\geq 1) gives  

 \zeta (2m,z)=\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(r+z)^{2m}}
         =\frac{1}{(2m-1)!}\,\psi^{(2m-1)}(z).

Inserting z=1/\alpha  into part (b) yields the exact identity

 I_{2m}(\alpha )=\frac{\alpha^{-2m}}{(2m-1)!}\,
             \psi^{(2m-1)}\!\Bigl(\frac1\alpha\Bigr)\!,  m\geq 1. (*)

Explicit cases.

m=2 (k=4).  Formula (*) gives  

 I_4(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-4}}{6}\,\psi^{(3)}\!\Bigl(\frac1\alpha\Bigr).

For \alpha =2, \psi ^{(3)}(\frac{1}{2})=\pi ^{4}, hence  

 I_4(2)=\frac{1}{16}\cdot\frac{\pi ^{4}}{6}=\dfrac{\pi ^{4}}{96}.

m=3 (k=6).  Formula (*) gives  

 I_6(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-6}}{120}\,\psi^{(5)}\!\Bigl(\frac1\alpha\Bigr).

For \alpha =2, \psi ^{(5)}(\frac{1}{2})=8\pi ^{6}, hence  

 I_6(2)=\frac{1}{64}\cdot\frac{8\pi ^{6}}{120}=\dfrac{\pi ^{6}}{960}.

Both results are positive, in perfect agreement with the elementary formula
I_{2m}(2)=(1-2^{-2m})\zeta (2m).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension: the problem generalises a 2-fold integral to an arbitrary k-fold one, forcing the solver to control a singularity on a codimension-k set and to justify term-wise integration in many variables.  

• Additional structures: ζ(k) and β(s) (Riemann zeta and Dirichlet beta) appear; knowledge of their special values and interplay with Γ(s) is required.  

• Parametric component: J(s) depends on a continuous parameter, demanding uniform convergence arguments and mastery of Mellin–type transforms.  

• Interacting concepts: geometric-series expansion, Fubini/Tonelli theorems, Gamma integrals, special values of L-functions, and high-order differentiation all occur and must be combined coherently.  

• Deeper theory: Part 3 asks for a non-trivial functional identity linking two ostensibly unrelated families of integrals via derivatives of β, pushing the solver into analytic number theory territory.  

The problem therefore requires substantially more sophisticated techniques and longer chains of reasoning than either the original exercise or the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  

(I) For every integer k \geq  2 set  
   I_k := \iint _{[0,1]^k} \frac{dx_1\cdots dx_k}{1-\bigl(x_1x_2\cdots x_k\bigr)^{\,2}}\!.

(II) For every real number s>0 set  
   J(s):=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{x^{\,s-1}\,dx}{e^{x}+e^{-x}}\!.

Throughout \Gamma  denotes the Gamma-function, \zeta  the Riemann zeta-function and  
\beta (s)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{n}}{(2n+1)^{s}} the Dirichlet beta-function.

1. (a) Prove that I_k converges for every k\geq 2.  
 (b) Show that  
   I_k=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(2n+1)^{k}}=(1-2^{-k})\,\zeta(k).  
  Give explicit numerical values for k=3 and k=4.

2. (a) Prove that J(s) converges for every s>0 and that  
   J(s)=\Gamma(s)\,\beta(s).  
 (b) Compute J(3) and J(5) in closed form.

 Fix a real parameter \alpha >0 and set  
   I_k(\alpha ):=\displaystyle\iint_{[0,1]^{k}}\frac{dx_1\cdots dx_k}{1-\bigl(x_1x_2\cdots x_k\bigr)^{\alpha}}\!.  

 (a) Show that I_k(\alpha ) converges for every k\geq 2.

 (b) Prove that  
   I_k(\alpha )=\alpha^{-k}\,\zeta\!\bigl(k,\tfrac1\alpha\bigr),  
  where \zeta (s,q)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(n+q)^{s}} is the Hurwitz zeta-function.

 (c) Deduce Part 1 (b) by putting \alpha =2 and using the identity  
   \zeta (k,\tfrac12)=(2^{k}-1)\,\zeta(k).

 (d)  Denote by \psi ^{(m)}(z)=\dfrac{d^{m+1}}{dz^{m+1}}\log\Gamma(z) the m-th polygamma function.  
  Show that for every even k=2m\geq 2 one has the exact formula  
   I_{2m}(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-2m}}{(2m-1)!}\;\psi^{(2m-1)}\!\Bigl(\frac1\alpha\Bigr). (*)

    \psi ^{(3)}\!\bigl(\tfrac12\bigr)=\pi^{4},  \psi ^{(5)}\!\bigl(\tfrac12\bigr)=8\pi^{6},  

  obtain the explicit closed forms  

    I_4(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-4}}{6}\;\psi^{(3)}\!\Bigl(\dfrac1\alpha\Bigr),  
    I_6(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-6}}{120}\;\psi^{(5)}\!\Bigl(\dfrac1\alpha\Bigr),  

  and verify in particular that  
   I_4(2)=\dfrac{\pi^{4}}{96}, I_6(2)=\dfrac{\pi^{6}}{960}.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Standard facts about the Gamma, zeta, beta, Hurwitz-zeta and polygamma functions are freely used.


1.  The basic integral I_k

1 (a) Convergence.  
Put P=(x_1x_2\cdots x_k)^2.  Over the unit cube one has 0\leq P\leq 1 and the only potential
singularity of the integrand 1/(1-P) occurs at the point (1,1,\ldots ,1) where P=1.
Write x_i=1-t_i with t_i\in [0,1].  Then

 1-P=1-\bigl(1-\sum_{i=1}^{k}t_i+O(|t|^{2})\bigr)=\sum_{i=1}^{k}t_i+O(|t|^{2}).

Hence near the singular point the integrand behaves like 1/\sum  t_i, whose
k-dimensional integral over a small cube is finite as soon as k\geq 2.
No other singularities exist, so I_k converges for every k\geq 2.

1 (b) Evaluation.  
Expand 1/(1-P)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}P^{n}, interchange
sum and integral (Tonelli) and compute

 I_k=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\prod_{j=1}^{k}\int_{0}^{1}x^{2n}\,dx
     =\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\Bigl(\frac1{2n+1}\Bigr)^{k}
     =(1-2^{-k})\,\zeta(k).

Numerical cases:

 k=3: I_3=(1-\tfrac18)\zeta(3)=\tfrac78\,\zeta(3).  
 k=4: I_4=(1-\tfrac1{16})\zeta(4)=\tfrac{15}{16}\cdot\frac{\pi^{4}}{90}
         =\dfrac{\pi^{4}}{96}.


2.  The integral  J(s)

2 (a) Convergence.  
Near x=0 we have e^{x}+e^{-x}=2+O(x^{2}), so the integrand behaves like
x^{s-1} and is integrable whenever s>0.
For x\to \infty  the denominator grows like e^{x}, so the integrand behaves like
x^{s-1}e^{-x}, again integrable for every s.

Series representation:
 \frac1{e^{x}+e^{-x}}
     =\frac{e^{-x}}{1+e^{-2x}}
     =\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{n}\,e^{-(2n+1)x},
uniformly on [\varepsilon ,\infty ) for any \varepsilon >0; thus

 J(s)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{n}\int_{0}^{\infty}x^{s-1}e^{-(2n+1)x}\,dx
     =\Gamma(s)\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{n}}{(2n+1)^{s}}
     =\Gamma(s)\,\beta(s).

2 (b) Special values.
 \beta (3)=\pi^{3}/32, \beta (5)=5\pi^{5}/1536,

 \Gamma (3)=2, \Gamma (5)=24,

so J(3)=\pi^{3}/16 and J(5)=5\pi^{5}/64.


3.  The parametrised integrals I_k(\alpha )

3 (a) Convergence.  
The only critical point is (1,\ldots ,1).  Put x_i=1-t_i (t_i\geq 0);
then

 1-\bigl(x_1x_2\cdots x_k\bigr)^{\alpha}
     =1-\exp\!\bigl(\alpha\sum_{i}\log(1-t_i)\bigr)
     =\alpha\sum_{i}t_i+O(|t|^{2}).

Thus the integrand behaves like 1/\sum  t_i, which is k-integrable for k\geq 2.

3 (b) Evaluation through the Hurwitz zeta.  
Expand

 \frac1{1-P^{\alpha}}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}P^{\alpha n}, P=x_1x_2\cdots x_k,

interchange sum and integral and obtain

 I_k(\alpha )=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\Bigl(\frac1{\alpha n+1}\Bigr)^{k}
         =\alpha^{-k}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(n+\frac1\alpha)^{k}}
         =\alpha^{-k}\,\zeta\!\bigl(k,\tfrac1\alpha\bigr).

3 (c) Specialisation \alpha =2.  
Because \zeta (k,\tfrac12)=(2^{k}-1)\,\zeta (k) we recover

 I_k(2)=2^{-k}(2^{k}-1)\,\zeta (k)=(1-2^{-k})\,\zeta (k)

in agreement with Part 1 (b).

3 (d) Even orders and the polygamma function.  
Recall the higher-order
polygamma: \psi ^{(m)}(z)=\dfrac{d^{m+1}}{dz^{m+1}}\log\Gamma(z).
For n\geq 0 one has  

 \psi ^{(n)}(z)=(-1)^{\,n+1}n!\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(r+z)^{\,n+1}}.

Taking n=2m-1 (m\geq 1) gives  

 \zeta (2m,z)=\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(r+z)^{2m}}
         =\frac{1}{(2m-1)!}\,\psi^{(2m-1)}(z).

Inserting z=1/\alpha  into part (b) yields the exact identity

 I_{2m}(\alpha )=\frac{\alpha^{-2m}}{(2m-1)!}\,
             \psi^{(2m-1)}\!\Bigl(\frac1\alpha\Bigr)\!,  m\geq 1. (*)

Explicit cases.

m=2 (k=4).  Formula (*) gives  

 I_4(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-4}}{6}\,\psi^{(3)}\!\Bigl(\frac1\alpha\Bigr).

For \alpha =2, \psi ^{(3)}(\frac{1}{2})=\pi ^{4}, hence  

 I_4(2)=\frac{1}{16}\cdot\frac{\pi ^{4}}{6}=\dfrac{\pi ^{4}}{96}.

m=3 (k=6).  Formula (*) gives  

 I_6(\alpha )=\dfrac{\alpha^{-6}}{120}\,\psi^{(5)}\!\Bigl(\frac1\alpha\Bigr).

For \alpha =2, \psi ^{(5)}(\frac{1}{2})=8\pi ^{6}, hence  

 I_6(2)=\frac{1}{64}\cdot\frac{8\pi ^{6}}{120}=\dfrac{\pi ^{6}}{960}.

Both results are positive, in perfect agreement with the elementary formula
I_{2m}(2)=(1-2^{-2m})\zeta (2m).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension: the problem generalises a 2-fold integral to an arbitrary k-fold one, forcing the solver to control a singularity on a codimension-k set and to justify term-wise integration in many variables.  

• Additional structures: ζ(k) and β(s) (Riemann zeta and Dirichlet beta) appear; knowledge of their special values and interplay with Γ(s) is required.  

• Parametric component: J(s) depends on a continuous parameter, demanding uniform convergence arguments and mastery of Mellin–type transforms.  

• Interacting concepts: geometric-series expansion, Fubini/Tonelli theorems, Gamma integrals, special values of L-functions, and high-order differentiation all occur and must be combined coherently.  

• Deeper theory: Part 3 asks for a non-trivial functional identity linking two ostensibly unrelated families of integrals via derivatives of β, pushing the solver into analytic number theory territory.  

The problem therefore requires substantially more sophisticated techniques and longer chains of reasoning than either the original exercise or the current kernel variant.
